narrative_blocks.header: Lived nearly fifty years in Madison Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Arah Norton was born in Guilford CT in 1739 and married Phebe Scranton of East Guilford in 1764. They settled at Madison — the village that East Guilford had become — and stayed there. Arah died in Madison in 1813 at seventy-four; Phebe followed five years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived nearly fifty years in Madison Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Arah Norton was born in Guilford CT in 1739 and married Phebe Scranton of East Guilford in 1764. They settled at Madison — the village that East Guilford had become — and stayed there. Arah died in Madison in 1813 at seventy-four; Phebe followed five years later.

parents.father_research_notes: Josiah Scranton of East Guilford (now Madison) CT. Per EH 1909 entry 398; not built per Jalapeño.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Dickenson Scranton of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 398; not built per Jalapeño.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest daughter omitted from EH's record
narrative_blocks.body: Rhoda Norton was born in 1763 — three years before her brother Josiah, the eldest child whom Edward Hooker named in his 1909 genealogy. WikiTree documents Rhoda's marriage to Zebul Norton in Waterbury in 1782 and four children. The reason for her omission from EH is not established; she may have been overlooked as a daughter who married out of Connecticut documentation.

parents.father_research_notes: David Norton — per WikiTree Norton-3155 records.
parents.mother_research_notes: Susa Bristol — David's second wife. WikiTree notes she possibly died at the birth of Cyrus on January 14, 1755; the next child was Susa b. July 12, 1759. Not built per Jalapeño.

military_service.unit: 3rd Battalion, Col. Roger Enos, Wooster's Brigade, Connecticut State Troops
military_service.notes: Private Zebul Norton served with Capt. James Peck's Company in the 3rd Battalion, Col. Roger Enos, Wooster's Brigade, Connecticut State Troops, during the American Revolution. Documented enlistment as Minute Men and volunteers, September 17, 1777.

narrative_blocks.header: Mustered as Private at age sixteen
narrative_blocks.body: Zebul Norton enlisted as a Private in Captain James Peck's Company, 3rd Battalion under Colonel Roger Enos in Wooster's Brigade of the Connecticut State Troops on September 17, 1777 — at age sixteen. He served the Revolutionary cause and returned home to Connecticut, where he married Rhoda Norton in 1782 and fathered four children before dying about 1803.

naming_inspiration.description: Birth-year naming for the United States Congress — born 1783, the year the Treaty of Paris ended the Revolutionary War.

military_service.notes: Veteran of the War of 1812. Specific unit and rank not yet established.

narrative_blocks.header: War of 1812 veteran moved family to Onondaga
narrative_blocks.body: Augustus Norton was born in Connecticut in 1785, the son of a Revolutionary veteran. He served in the War of 1812 and moved his young family to Onondaga County, New York, where he farmed and raised at least one documented son. He died at Onondaga Hill in 1859.

narrative_blocks.header: Returned to Guilford in widowhood
narrative_blocks.body: Hannah Norton Hotchkiss married Amos Hotchkiss in East Guilford on December 12, 1791, and they raised their family in Wallingford CT. Amos died in 1808 at forty-three; Hannah lived another forty-three years and died in Guilford — her natal town — in 1851.

parents.father_research_notes: Amos Hotchkiss Sr. of Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1040.
parents.mother_research_notes: Desire Dowd Hotchkiss of Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1040.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Truman Benedict on Independence Day
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Hotchkiss married Truman Benedict on July 4, 1819 in Connecticut — an Independence Day wedding. Across twenty years they had ten children, including three sons all named Truman who each died in infancy or at age two. Elizabeth died in 1867; Truman outlived her by thirteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons named Truman all died young
narrative_blocks.body: The Benedict household lost three sons in succession all named Truman: the first (born 1821) died at age two in 1823; the second (born 1828) died the same year; the third (born 1831) also died the same year. Elizabeth and Truman tried three times to give their family the father's name and three times lost the child.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married the same Dowd
narrative_blocks.body: Mabel Norton's daughters Adelia and Phebe both married Harvey H. Dowd — a sororate marriage in which Phebe married her sister's widower after Adelia's death. The pattern was common in nineteenth-century New England when widowers needed help with young children.

parents.father_research_notes: Beriah Norton of Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1042.
parents.mother_research_notes: Rebecca Dowd Norton of Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1042.

narrative_blocks.header: Lorenzo died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lorenzo died in 1832 at the age of fifteen.

career.notes: Self-made farmer. Cleared land and built a log cabin upon arrival in Addison County VT about 1800. Owned 500 acres at the time of his death in 1833. The homestead was inherited by his son Hiram Norton.

narrative_blocks.header: Cleared Vermont homestead at age twenty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Abel Norton was born in Connecticut in 1777 and migrated to Addison County, Vermont about 1800. He cleared land, built a log cabin, and farmed there the rest of his life. By his 1833 death he owned five hundred acres and was called in the local history 'a self-made man.' His son Hiram inherited the homestead.

narrative_blocks.header: Raised ten children eight survived to adulthood
narrative_blocks.body: Abel and Mehitable raised ten children at the Vermont homestead. Per the 19th-century Addison County local history, eight of the ten survived to adulthood — a high rate for the early-1800s frontier. Two children are presently identified: Calvin and Hiram.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to ninety-five in Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Norton was born in Guilford CT in 1765 and married Abel Norton — twelve years her junior. They migrated together to Addison County Vermont about 1800, where she bore ten children. After Abel's 1833 death she returned to her natal Guilford and lived there twenty-seven more years, dying January 8, 1860 at age approximately ninety-five.

career.notes: Purchased a farm in Essex County NY where he remained twelve years.
Returned to Addison County VT in 1847 and purchased the Goodale farm — 166 acres with a residence built by Jared Goodale at an early date.
Moved to Weybridge VT in 1854 and bought the Lawrence place — 50 acres. Owned multiple farms in Addison County aggregating more than 500 acres.

narrative_blocks.header: Owned five hundred acres across Addison County
narrative_blocks.body: Calvin Norton spent his life consolidating Vermont farmland. He bought a farm in Essex County NY for twelve years; in 1847 bought the 166-acre Goodale farm in Addison County; in 1854 moved to Weybridge and bought the 50-acre Lawrence place. At peak he owned over five hundred acres across multiple Addison County farms — matching the size of his father Abel's original homestead.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at seventy-three to Jane's birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Calvin's first wife Sarah Sargeant died November 1884 after forty-nine years of marriage. Four months later, on March 19, 1885 — Jane Sargent's forty-third birthday — Calvin married her, daughter of Samuel Sargent of Middlebury. Calvin was seventy-three; Jane was thirty years his junior.

narrative_blocks.header: Three children only Calvin Jr survived
narrative_blocks.body: Calvin and Sarah Sargeant had three children — one daughter and two sons. Only the youngest son, Calvin Junior, survived to adulthood. The local history does not name the two children who died young.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at fifty-one in Guilford
narrative_blocks.body: Diadema Norton Murray died July 10, 1837 in Guilford CT at age fifty-one — twenty-seven years after the death of her husband Calvin Murray. She had been widowed at twenty-five with three young children all under five, of whom her youngest Beulah Maria was born just six weeks before Calvin's 1810 death.

parents.father_research_notes: John Murray of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1044.
parents.mother_research_notes: Mindwell Crampton Murray of East Guilford CT. Per EH 1909 entry 1044.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-nine leaving widow with three
narrative_blocks.body: Calvin Murray died at East Guilford on November 14, 1810 at age twenty-nine, leaving his wife Diadema Norton Murray a widow at twenty-four. They had three young children: Dickenson aged five, Calvin Nelson aged two, and Beulah Maria born just six weeks before her father's death.

naming_inspiration.description: Given his great-grandmother Mary Dickenson Scranton's surname as a first name — Mary was the mother of Phebe Scranton Norton.
Given his father Calvin Murray's name — his father had died when Calvin Nelson was only two years old.

burial.plot_notes: West Cemetery, Madison (formerly East Guilford), New Haven, Connecticut

parents.father_research_notes: Thomas Norton of Guilford (b. Mar 4, 1675; d. Sep 21, 1740) — miller and wheelwright

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)
Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

notable.notable_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate, 1703
notable.primary_url_label: Historic Buildings CT — Dr. Daniel Hooker House 1725

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor, B.A. graduate in 1703
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first tutor appointed at the Collegiate School (Yale) by first rector Abraham Pierson Jr. — Yale's earliest faculty member. He received his Yale B.A. in 1703, alongside Rev. John Hart who was the first student to graduate.

narrative_blocks.header: Surgeon of the Canada expedition, 1711
narrative_blocks.body: He served as surgeon of the Massachusetts expedition against Canada in 1711 — the final major military operation of Queen Anne's War. He then returned to Wethersfield to practice medicine and law.

narrative_blocks.header: Declined his father's pulpit — chose medicine
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died in 1697, Farmington invited Daniel to succeed his father. He declined the pulpit and chose medicine — studying law alongside it, not to practice but to help patients make their wills.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington-born man with a college education
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Harvard in 1700, then enrolled at the new Collegiate School in Saybrook to receive its B.A. in 1703. He was the first Farmington-born man to obtain a college education.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited and divided Thomas Hooker's West Hartford farm
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited Thomas Hooker's farm in West Hartford and in 1728 deeded it to his sons. The land had been part of the original Hooker grant — passing through three generations from founder to surgeon.

institutions.institution_blurb: First Yale tutor 1701-1704; first B.A. graduate 1703.

cross_connections.display_label: He tutored under Pierson — Yale's first rector.
His niece married John Hart, his Yale successor
His successor as Yale tutor — second graduate

career.notes: First tutor of Yale Collegiate School. Hired by rector Abraham Pierson Jr. (X02127). Resigned in favor of Rev. John Hart (I00137).
Settled Wethersfield after leaving Yale. Also studied law to help patients make wills — no evidence he practiced law professionally.

education.notes: B.A. Harvard 1700. First Farmington-born man to receive a college education.

Received degree at Yale's first commencement 1703. EH says he was "the first B.A. of Yale" but also that Rev. John Hart (I00137) was "nominally the second, but really the first actual student to graduate." Daniel was the tutor/faculty; Hart was the first student B.A. Conflict flagged — see research_notes.

bio.title: Doctor
bio.bio_blurb: Yale's first tutor and first B.A. graduate; later a Wethersfield physician

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America (1887)

sources.notes: Wethersfield Village Cemetery; carved by William Stanclift

burial.plot_notes: Marker type: gravestone. Original marker survives. Carved by William Stanclift. One of the older surviving carved gravestones in the region.
